beats.prompt: Quick recall. From the counterfeit-coin teaser, match each setup to its count.

feedback.correct: 3³ = 27 combos; a balance gives 3 answers per weighing — left, balanced, or right — so each weighing carries a base-3 digit.
hints: Each weighing has three outcomes, not two.
Left / balanced / right = a base-3 digit; 3 bags × 3 states = 3³ = 27.
Match: 3 bags × 3 states → 3³=27; balance outcomes → left/balanced/right; digits per weighing → 1.

beats.prompt: 12 coins, exactly one is fake — heavier OR lighter, you don't know which. Using a balance scale, what is the minimum number of weighings needed to find the fake coin AND say whether it is heavy or light?

6.note: Worth testing — you're halving like the mice; a scale gives three answers, so split into thirds instead of halves.

4.note: Worth testing — 3³ = 27 outcomes already exceed the 25 you must distinguish (2·12+1 = 25), so 3 weighings suffice.

3.note: Right — a balance is base-3, so split into thirds: 3 weighings.

beats.prompt: One idea to keep in mind.

interaction.title: A balance is base-3, not base-2
interaction.body: A yes/no question gives 2 answers (binary). A balance scale gives 3 answers: left pan heavier, balanced, or right pan heavier. Each weighing is therefore a base-3 digit, carrying ≈ 1.58 bits of information — more than a yes/no test.

feedback.correct: Left / balanced / right = three answers = one base-3 digit per weighing.
hints: Count the outcomes: a scale always tilts left, stays level, or tilts right.
Three outcomes = one base-3 digit.
Read it, then tap Continue.

beats.prompt: 9 coins — one is heavier than the rest (you already know the direction). What is the fewest number of weighings needed to find the heavy coin?

fields.label: Minimum weighings to find 1 heavy coin among 9

feedback.correct: Exactly — three groups of 3: one weighing per base-3 digit. ⌈log₃9⌉ = 2 weighings.
hints: Split 9 into 3 equal groups; put one group on each pan. The tilt picks the group holding the heavy coin.
One weighing per base-3 digit: split the survivors into thirds again. Two weighings total.
The answer is 2.

beats.prompt: Load the pans. Drag coin groups onto the two pans — the beam tilts left, stays level, or tilts right (the 3-way digit). Split suspects into thirds and the tilt picks the third. How many weighings does 12 coins need?

feedback.correct: Each 3-way tilt eliminates two-thirds of the suspects. 12 coins needs 3 weighings.
hints: Split into 3 equal groups, not 2. Put one group on each pan and leave one group off.
The tilt names which third holds the fake — eliminating 8 coins in one weighing.
12 coins → 3 weighings, since 3³ = 27 ≥ 2·12+1 = 25 outcomes to distinguish.

beats.interviewNote: The 12-coin and Bachet-weights puzzles are classic quant/brain-teaser questions. The unlock is recognizing the *alphabet* of the test — a balance has three letters, so think base-3, just as a yes/no test thinks base-2.
beats.prompt: Generalize: N items, each weighing gives 3 outcomes. See how ⌈log₃N⌉ compares to ⌈log₂N⌉, and why unknown heavy-or-light costs extra slack.

feedback.correct: 12 coins, direction unknown → 3 weighings: (3³−3)/2 = 12. Direction known → ⌈log₃12⌉ = 3 also, but the bound is tighter. A yes/no test would need ⌈log₂25⌉ = 5 questions for the same task.
hints: A balance has 3 outcomes per step vs 2 for yes/no — so ⌈log₃N⌉ instead of ⌈log₂N⌉.
Unknown direction (heavier or lighter?) doubles the outcomes: 2N + 1 total possibilities, so the bound is (3ⁿ−3)/2 ≥ N.

beats.prompt: Bachet's problem: you have a two-pan balance and can place weights on either pan. What is the smallest set of integer weights that can measure every whole-number mass from 1 to 40 grams? Enter the weights as a comma-separated list, or just the largest weight.

fields.label: Weight set covering 1–40 g (comma list, or just the largest)

feedback.correct: Yes — powers of 3: {1, 3, 9, 27}. Weights on either pan give every mass 1–40 (1+3+9+27 = 40). This is balanced ternary, with digits −1, 0, +1.
hints: You may place weights with the object (subtract). Putting a weight on the same pan as the mass is the same as subtracting it.
That makes base-3: each weight is either opposite (adds), off (ignores), or with the object (subtracts). You need powers of 3.
The set is {1, 3, 9, 27}: 1+3+9+27 = 40, covering every mass 1–40.

beats.prompt: Held-out transfer: using weights {1, 3, 9, 27} and a two-pan balance, how do you weigh a 22 g object? Describe the placement as an equation (e.g. 27−9+3+1), or just enter 22 to confirm you know the mass.

feedback.correct: 22 = 27−9+3+1: put 27 opposite the object, 9 on the same pan as the object, 3 and 1 opposite. Balanced-ternary digits of 22 over {27,9,3,1}: +1,−1,+1,+1.
hints: You may place weights with the object (a negative contribution). Try 27 on the far pan.
27 − 9 = 18; add 3 and 1 on the far pan: 18+3+1 = 22. So 9 goes with the object.
Placement: 27−9+3+1 = 22. The balanced-ternary representation of 22.

beats.prompt: Prove you have it. You have 12 coins and a balance scale. Exactly one coin is fake — it may be either heavier or lighter than a genuine coin, and you do not know which. What is the minimum number of weighings guaranteed to identify the fake coin and determine whether it is heavy or light?

interaction.scenario: 12 coins, one fake (heavier or lighter — direction unknown). Balance scale. Guarantee the fake's identity and direction.

feedback.correct: 3 weighings. Each weighing gives 3 outcomes, so 3 weighings yield 3³ = 27 outcome sequences. You need to distinguish 2·12+1 = 25 possibilities (12 coins × 2 directions + the genuine result). The GB bound: (3ⁿ−3)/2 ≥ N → (27−3)/2 = 12 ≥ 12. ✓
hints: Direction unknown means the fake could be heavier OR lighter — that doubles the outcomes you must distinguish: 2×12+1 = 25.
Use the bound (3ⁿ−3)/2 ≥ N. For n=3: (27−3)/2 = 12 ≥ 12. So 3 weighings suffice.
The answer is 3.

feedback.correct: A balance speaks base-3: three answers per weighing means ⌈log₃N⌉ weighings identify one defective among N (or ⌈log₃((3ⁿ−3)/2)⌉ when direction is unknown). Powers of 3 — {1,3,9,27} — measure every mass 1–40 via balanced ternary (digits −1, 0, +1). Next: bit tricks.
hints: Each weighing is a base-3 digit: 3 outcomes, not 2.
Unknown direction needs (3ⁿ−3)/2 ≥ N; known direction needs 3ⁿ ≥ N.
Bachet weights are powers of 3; balanced ternary digits ∈ {−1, 0, +1} place weights on either pan.
